artifacts.content:
Hi Mum,

Kids are finally in bed and Michael’s on night shift again, so I’m catching up on life admin with a cuppa. The shop’s ticking along – tourist season is starting to ramp up, and I’m already stressing about running out of gas bottles over Easter.

You asked where exactly the new sign is going. It’ll be fixed right above the main doors at 142 Newman Drive, centred on that 6m-wide masonry panel they re-rendered in November 2022 after the Shire had a go at me about the mould and water staining. That was when I was down in Bunbury with you for chemo at Bunbury Regional, remember? I still feel guilty I wasn’t here to deal with it, but the wall actually looks better now. I was texting Michael tonight while I was filling out the Shire’s application form and told him I’d tick “permanent” for the duration if they’ll let me, and if they won’t, I’ll just write in whatever the maximum period is so I don’t have to reapply every five minutes.

It’s also the same bit of wall where they replaced the glass after Michael backed the Prado into the shopfront in March 2023 when he was rushing to grab Ethan from Newman Primary during that asthma attack. The new sign will sit just above that repaired section, still flush with the façade so it doesn’t stick out or mess with the security cameras we upgraded after the July 2021 firearm break‑in that Senior Constable Porter attended.

I’ve gone with that deep charcoal grey again – same as the repaint after the August 10, 2023 break‑in – so it matches what shows up on the Newman Police CCTV footage if anything ever happens. Text will be white and light grey, with the burnt orange like the DV raffle ribbons from 15 October 2022. It actually looks really classy, not cheap.

Anyway, how are you feeling after your last check-up? Has Bunbury Community Health booked the next counselling block yet?

Love,
Danielle

metadata.subject: Where the new sign is going + how you’re doing

artifacts.content:
Hey Kate,

Finally sat down after doing stocktake on fridges and swags – my back is killing me. Thought I’d answer your question about why I’m being so fussy with the sign colours and materials.

The sign guy probably thinks I’m over the top, but there’s actually logic behind it. I’m keeping that muted sky blue from Ethan’s sensory corner – the one his psychologist at Newman Family Practice picked in March 2024 for his anxiety space. It’s the same blue we painted behind his beanbag, and he really does seem calmer with it, so I figured having it in the logo might make the shop feel a bit more welcoming for families.

Frame is going matte black, same as the grab rails and step edges we put in after Sophie’s occupational therapist at Perth Children’s Hospital did the access recommendations back in July 2021. I like that everything ties together visually, but it also means she can still use those strong contrast markings she relies on when she’s tired.

Material-wise I’m going with low‑gloss ACP and a welded galvanised frame, partly because of Ethan’s photosensitive migraines – remember when Dr Priya Nair at Newman Hospital diagnosed that in May 2022? I don’t want some shiny thing throwing glare into his eyes when he walks past from Newman Primary at 3 pm.

Money is still tight after falling two months behind on the NAB overdraft last August–September, so I’m sticking with the same charcoal / burnt orange / blue palette I sent the BHP property guy on 3 May 2023 when I cut night trading from burnout. I can’t afford another $850 rebrand if Michael’s Mt Whaleback roster changes again.

How’s work at the clinic? Still drowning in knees and backs?

Love,
Dani

metadata.subject: Why I’m being picky about the sign colours

artifacts.content:
Business planning – signage + money/health

1. Materials & security
- After the 14 March 2024 break‑in at Pilbara Outdoor & Camping Supplies (front glass door smashed, cash drawer gone), I promised myself no more flimsy stuff. Go with 3mm ACP panel, anti‑graffiti laminate, fully welded 40mm galv steel frame. Use stainless chem‑set anchors and tamper‑proof screws – exactly what Mark Ellison from RAC talked through on 20 March when he did the assessment for the $7,800 claim. If I’m spending this much, the sign needs to feel like it’s there for the long haul, same as me – Danielle Louise Mercer, 38, still planning to be opening the doors at 142 Newman Drive for a long time yet.
- Dust storms: remember November 2023 when the old corflute sign tore off and nearly hit that ute? Ranger Paul Henderson came out to 142 Newman Drive on 18/11/23 and basically said, “If that had hit someone, you’d be in trouble.” So: no more corflute, everything fixed solidly into the masonry.

2. Maintenance & Dad
- Don’t forget Dad’s ladder fall in January 2021 when we were repainting the front. That ED visit at Newman Hospital scared everyone. Choose materials that only need a wipe‑down every few months – no timber that needs sanding and repainting every 12–18 months. No more ladder jobs for him, ever.

3. Positioning idea (early draft)
- First thought ages ago was a freestanding frame right near the corner of Newman Drive, pushed as close to the road as possible so cars couldn’t miss it. That corner is basically bare red dirt and a couple of old pallets where we dump excess stock. Would’ve stuck the sign just in front of the low fence and let it poke above the roofline so you could see it from both directions. Probably overkill/too risky – stick with the wall‑mounted plan. When I talk to Shire of East Pilbara about the approval, ask straight out: can you just approve it for as long as the business is trading there, even if that’s decades, so I’m not re‑doing paperwork every few years?

4. To‑do
- Get updated quote from Luke at Perth Sign & Print.
- File invoices with insurance paperwork.
- Block out install day so I can be on site.
- Double‑check any conditions in the approval so future‑Danielle isn’t blindsided if we’re still there in 2035 and they suddenly want changes.

Hi Sarah,

Hope you’re well. Just wanted to give you an update on the shop, since you’ve basically become my unofficial business therapist.

I’m finally sorting a proper front sign. After the August 10, 2023 break‑in (the one where Newman Police pulled the CCTV footage and Senior Constable Harris kept rewinding the bit where they jimmied the back door), I repainted the fascia in Colorbond Monument charcoal so the cameras pick up the “Pilbara Outdoor & Camping Supplies” wording clearly. The new sign will use the same deep charcoal background with white and light grey text so if we ever have another incident, the name reads clearly on footage. I was filling out the Shire of East Pilbara form yesterday and there’s a question about how long the approval should run for – I’m leaning toward ticking whatever box means it’s basically approved indefinitely so I don’t have to muck around reapplying in a few years.

I’m also trying to simplify lighting. Remember when the cops turned up at 11:30 pm on 22 December 2022 because neighbours complained that my high‑intensity LED spotlight on the front wall was blinding them and distracting drivers heading to the Mt Whaleback turn‑off? Between that and Dr Patel at Newman Hospital telling us on 14 March 2023 that bright lights were triggering Ethan’s migraines after my late‑night trading, I’ve decided the new sign won’t have any dedicated lighting at all. It’ll just sit under the existing low‑level wall lights and whatever the streetlights give us, which seems more than enough for a little shop on Newman Drive.

Lisa at NAB Newman also gave me a serve last 5 September about the power bill jumping $480 between July and September 2023 because I kept that 200‑watt floodlight on until 10 pm every night. With the overdraft already nudging the $25k limit, I can’t justify lighting it up like Optus Stadium.

Anyway, enough shop talk. How are the kids going with soccer in this heat?

Cheers,
Danielle
142 Newman Drive
Newman WA 6753
Owner‑operator, Pilbara Outdoor & Camping Supplies

metadata.subject: Sign, lights and trying not to fry everyone’s eyeballs

artifacts.content:
Hi Mum,

Just finished the Sunday video call and realised I didn’t really answer your question about how long I plan to stay in this shop.

In a perfect world I’d like the new sign to last the whole 10‑year lease at 142 Newman Drive. Once Michael’s child support enforcement order from the Family Court in Perth kicks in from July 2025, they’ll be taking $650 a fortnight straight out of his Mt Whaleback wages. Between that, Ethan’s ADHD stuff, and Sophie’s therapies, there’s just no spare money for big cosmetic changes.

Ethan’s ADHD diagnosis at Newman Medical Centre last March has been a bit of a juggle. We’re paying $280 a month for psychology with Dr Karen Lewis in Perth, plus his anxiety sessions up here and Sophie’s speech therapy. That’s why I put in my business plan that the signage needs to see us through at least the next 8–10 years, ideally until Ethan finishes Year 12. I can’t be ripping down and redoing the front every time I get the itch to “freshen things up.”

On top of that, we’re still watching Michael’s performance management plan at BHP. It runs until December 2027, and if anything went wrong there, the shop would be our only income. So I’m trying to make decisions now that assume we won’t be able to touch the sign again for at least 5–7 years.

I keep thinking about you maybe moving up after your second hip replacement at St John of God Bunbury in early 2026. If I’m your main carer for a while, the last thing I’ll have time for is arguing with tradies about fonts.

Love you,
Danielle

metadata.subject: How long I’m planning to stay put here

artifacts.content:
Dani: You’ll laugh at this now but I nearly cried at school pickup today.

Kate: Oh no, what happened?

Dani: Mrs Harris caught me in the car park to remind me (again) about the time in Jan 2023 when Ethan and Sophie were standing on milk crates out the front of the shop waving those hand‑painted SALE signs. She said the P&C still jokes about my “human billboards.”

Kate: I remember that! The kids loved it.

Dani: Yeah, until the complaint email landed. That’s when I panicked and threw up the “OPEN 7 DAYS – CAMPING GEAR” vinyl banner instead. I told her I’m finally getting a proper wall sign so once that’s up, the banner will come down and there’ll be no more kids on crates.

Kate: Probably for the best. How’s Ethan with all the migraine stuff now?

Dani: Better since we took down that huge LED floodlight over the fascia after Dr Patel at Newman Hospital said on 14/3/23 it was likely triggering his attacks during my late trading nights. New sign will have no lights, just the existing wall ones and street lamps.

Kate: Good call.

Dani: Also need to stop being dodgy with the other bits – I promised myself that once I’ve got a decent front sign, I’ll stop leaving those two A‑frame boards out on the verge and median strip. I know they’re technically not meant to be there. One big, legit sign is less stressful than sneaking around with portable ones.

Kate: Adulting, hey.

Dani: Exactly. Anyway, how’s your week of back‑to‑back patients going?

To‑do – signage clean‑up + family stuff

1. Temporary banners / dodgy signs
- Pull down the vinyl “OPEN 7 DAYS – CAMPING GEAR” banner Jack helped me cable‑tie to the front awning that Saturday in March 2023 before his Perth flight. We were rushing because Michael had been called in for an extra night shift at Mt Whaleback after that near‑miss in the pit, and Jack really shouldn’t have been doing cash work between swings. I promised Michael I’d sort it myself this time.
- Also need to finally remove the banner Mum wired me $3,000 for back in late October 2022, when the bank in Newman knocked back my top‑up loan after seeing Michael’s old credit card default. I told her it was only a stopgap until I could afford a proper sign.
- Don’t forget the $260 rush‑print banner I ordered online while down in Perth at the Royal Perth Hospital sleep clinic with Dr Nguyen in early Feb 2023. That was pure insomnia/anxiety panic about people not seeing the shop from Newman Drive.
- Once the new wall sign is in, I should pull down that big DIY roof sign I bolted up myself last year. Pretty sure it wouldn’t pass any structural check and I don’t want it flying off in a storm.

2. Kids
- Remind Ethan’s school counsellor about his anxiety corner colours (muted sky blue).
- Check Sophie’s Tuesday dance term dates against Michael’s 4‑on/4‑off roster.

3. Mum & Dad
- Call Dad about his leg – make sure he’s not climbing any ladders.
- Check in with Mum about early 2026 hip replacement plans.

From: Danielle Mercer <danielle@pilbaraoutdoor.com>
To: Planning Approvals <planning@eastpilbarashire.wa.gov.au>
Date: 2026-02-18
Subject: Additional details – Pilbara Outdoor & Camping Supplies signage application

Hi team,

Following up on my application for the new wall sign at Pilbara Outdoor & Camping Supplies, I’ve pulled together the extra details you asked for.

The advertisement will be fixed to the front façade of my shop at 142 Newman Drive, Newman WA 6753, directly above the main entry doors where the old hand-painted “Mercer’s Guns & Ammo” signage used to be before we covered it up in July 2021 after that firearm break‑in incident that Senior Constable Rachel Porter from Newman Police Station attended. The new panel will sit centrally on the same 6.0 m x 2.0 m masonry panel facing Newman Drive, fully within the property boundary and clear of the existing awning and the security cameras that were upgraded after the burglary. It’s also aligned over the two customer bays closest to the rear laneway where I had CCTV installed in May 2023, after we caught one of my son Ethan’s classmates from Newman Primary School trying to tag the brickwork with spray paint.

There will be no separate light source for the sign. It will only be visible under the existing low-level wall lights and ambient street lighting. After my husband Michael’s fatigue-management session with his supervisor at the BHP Mt Whaleback mine in August 2023, they flagged that the very bright LED sign I’d trialled at the shop was drawing his attention and keeping him overstimulated when he drove past 142 Newman Drive after his 7:00 pm–7:00 am night shifts, so I’ve deliberately gone with a non-illuminated option.

Kind regards,
Danielle Louise Mercer
Owner, Pilbara Outdoor & Camping Supplies

metadata.subject: Additional details – Pilbara Outdoor & Camping Supplies signage application

artifacts.content:
From: Danielle Mercer <danielle@pilbaraoutdoor.com>
To: Katherine Collins <kate.collins@example.com>
Date: 2023-09-06
Subject: Sign saga + money stress (keeping you in the loop)

Hey Kate,

Quick download while the kids are at Newman Primary School and the shop’s quiet.

You honestly saved my bacon putting me onto Luke Harris at Perth Sign & Print in Osborne Park. After we fell two months behind on the Pilbara Outdoor & Camping Supplies NAB business overdraft in August–September 2023, I was freaking out about pushing it past the $40,000 limit. Luke’s $1,200 mates’ rate on the 3 mm aluminium composite panel, UV-stable vinyl graphics, anti-graffiti laminate and powder-coated steel frame (all sorted on 5 September 2023) means I can still get the sign up without tipping us over. I’ve parked the idea of the more expensive solid aluminium until cash flow improves.

The sign itself will be fixed flush to the front façade above the main entrance at 142 Newman Drive, directly over the car bay we’ve marked with yellow paint as “Reserved – Collins Family” since February 2024 so Dad (Peter John Collins) can park close to the door when he visits from Bunbury with his mobility issues after his minor stroke. I asked the installer to line it up centrally with that bay and the entry doors so it faces west to Newman Drive but doesn’t project over the road reserve or obstruct the adjoining tenancy.

I’m also planning to remove the vinyl banner I put up in mid-November 2022 once the Shire signs this off. That’s the same one Michael ducked out from Mt Whaleback to rehang in his lunch break after the Pilbara wind ripped a corner loose, which led to that disciplinary meeting with his supervisor about doing odd jobs for me in BHP time. I’m not risking that again.

Love,
Dani

metadata.subject: Sign saga + money stress (keeping you in the loop)

artifacts.content:
Notes to self:

- Height Above Ground Level To Top Of Advertisement: 4.2
- Height Above Ground Level To Underside: 3.0
